requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must maximize 401(k) employee salary deferral contributions including catch-up contributions when age 50+ and additional super catch-up when age 60-63 to reduce taxable income and build tax-deferred retirement savings
criteria: Must be eligible employee in employer's 401(k) plan meeting plan participation requirements
Age 50+ catch-up contribution: Additional $7,500 (total $31,000 if age 50-59 or 64+)
NEW: Age 60-63 super catch-up: Additional $11,250 for 2025 (total $34,750 for ages 60-63)
Contributions reduce current taxable income dollar-for-dollar (traditional 401(k)) or grow tax-free (Roth 401(k))
Can split contributions between traditional (pre-tax) and Roth (after-tax) 401(k) within aggregate limits
Catch-up contributions available starting year employee turns 50 (super catch-up starts at age 60)
Consider contribution timing, investment allocation, and traditional vs Roth strategy based on tax situation

401k_contribution_rules.age_60_63_advantage: Enhanced super catch-up $11,250 (vs regular $7,500) for ages 60-63 only
401k_contribution_rules.traditional_deduction: Pre-tax contributions reduce current taxable income and FICA wages
401k_contribution_rules.roth_option: After-tax Roth contributions provide tax-free growth and distributions in retirement
401k_contribution_rules.immediate_tax_savings_score: Traditional contributions save at marginal rate (up to 37%) plus 3.8% NIIT if applicable
401k_contribution_rules.employer_match: Employer matching contributions additional (not counted toward employee deferral limits)
401k_contribution_rules.combined_limit: Total contributions (employee + employer) limited to lesser of $70,000 (2025) or 100% compensation

restrictions: Super catch-up only available ages 60-63; reverts to regular $7,500 catch-up at age 64+
High earners may face nondiscrimination testing potentially limiting contributions
Highly compensated employees ($155,000+ for 2024) may have additional restrictions under ADP testing
Roth 401(k) contributions don't reduce current taxable income but grow tax-free
Cannot access funds before age 59½ without 10% early withdrawal penalty (with exceptions)
Required minimum distributions (RMDs) begin at age 73 (75 if born 1960+)
Roth 401(k) subject to RMDs unlike Roth IRA; consider in-service conversion to Roth IRA if available
State tax treatment varies; some states don't provide deduction for pre-tax contributions
